zh:
七千余座岛屿从未共享过一部神谱。吕宋的他加禄人称造物主为巴塔拉，米沙鄢海域听见巴库纳瓦吞月，棉兰老的马拉瑙人把萨里马诺克绘在王屋与船首；迪瓦塔一词在不同地方又可指神、祖灵、山林主人或受祭的自然力量。西班牙编年者、十九世纪民俗学者与二十世纪收藏家留下了大量记录，也常用基督教、欧洲妖魔学与殖民分类去重写所见之物。因此，本馆把「菲律宾群岛神话」当作一片多声部的星群，不伪装成一座从来存在的统一万神殿。

这些故事至今仍藏在日常禁忌里。大树下经过要低声请求看不见的主人让路，午后的山径可能被蒂克巴朗折叠成无尽回环，烟草气会先于卡普雷从榕树冠落下。阿斯旺并非单一物种，而是一张包住食尸者、巫者与夜行掠食者的恐惧之网；其中马纳南加尔在夜里让上半身生翼，留下最脆弱的下半身等待黎明。另一方面，吞月的巨蛇也不是只为恐吓：锣鼓、锅盆与呐喊把社群聚在同一轮被咬暗的月下。群岛神话最迷人的地方，正在于海上交通、地方语言与殖民历史不断改写彼此，却没有抹去黑夜里那一句古老的「请让我们经过」。
en:
More than seven thousand islands never shared a single divine genealogy. Tagalog communities of Luzon named Bathala as creator; Visayan seas heard the Bakunawa swallowing the moon; Maranao artists of Mindanao set the Sarimanok on royal houses and prows. Diwata may mean a deity, an ancestor, a keeper of forest or mountain, or a natural power approached with offerings, depending on who is speaking. Spanish chroniclers, nineteenth-century folklorists, and twentieth-century collectors preserved an immense record while also filtering it through Christianity, European demonology, and colonial categories. This atlas therefore treats Philippine mythology as a many-voiced constellation, not a unified pantheon that never existed.

The stories remain alive inside ordinary cautions. A traveler passing an old tree asks its unseen owners for leave; a tikbalang can fold the noon road into a loop; the smell of tobacco may fall from a balete crown before the kapre is seen. Aswang is not one species but a net of dread cast over corpse-eaters, witches, and nocturnal predators, while the manananggal grows wings from a severed torso and leaves its helpless lower half waiting for dawn. The moon-eating serpent offers another kind of terror: gongs, pans, and shouting gather the community beneath the same bitten moon. The archipelago’s lore draws its power from sea routes, local languages, and colonial histories continuously rewriting one another without silencing the old request whispered into darkness: let us pass.
